abstract: Generalization is a central challenge in autonomous driving, as real-world deployment requires robust performance under unseen scenarios, sensor domains, and environmental conditions. Recent world-model-based planning methods have shown strong capabilities in scene understanding and multi-modal future prediction, yet their generalization across datasets and sensor configurations remains limited. In addition, their loosely coupled planning paradigm often leads to poor video-trajectory consistency during visual imagination. To overcome these limitations, we propose DriveVA, a novel autonomous driving world model that jointly decodes future visual forecasts and action sequences in a shared latent generative process. DriveVA inherits rich priors on motion dynamics and physical plausibility from well-pretrained large-scale video generation models to capture continuous spatiotemporal evolution and causal interaction patterns. To this end, DriveVA employs a DiT-based decoder to jointly predict future action sequences (trajectories) and videos, enabling tighter alignment between planning and scene evolution. We also introduce a video continuation strategy to strengthen long-duration rollout consistency. DriveVA achieves an impressive closed-loop performance of 90.9 PDM score on the challenge NAVSIM. Extensive experiments also demonstrate the zero-shot capability and cross-domain generalization of DriveVA, which reduces average L2 error and collision rate by 78.9% and 83.3% on nuScenes and 52.5% and 52.4% on the Bench2drive built on CARLA v2 compared with the state-of-the-art world-model-based planner.

citations.fullCitation: Wan, T., Wang, A., Ai, B., Wen, B., Mao, C., Xie, C.W., Chen, D., et al.: Wan: Open and advanced large-scale video generative models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.20314 (2025)
citations.justification: This paper is fundamentally important as it provides the pre-trained video generation backbone (Wan2.2-TI2V-5B) upon which DriveVA is built. DriveVA leverages the rich spatiotemporal priors from this model to enable its strong generalization capabilities, making this citation a cornerstone of the proposed method.
citations.title: Wan: Open and advanced large-scale video generative models

citations.fullCitation: Zhao, Z., Fu, T., Wang, Y., Wang, L., Lu, H.: From forecasting to planning: Policy world model for collaborative state-action prediction. arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.19654 (2025)
citations.justification: This citation, referred to as PWM, represents a key state-of-the-art competitor that DriveVA is directly and repeatedly compared against, particularly for zero-shot generalization. The paper uses PWM as the primary baseline to demonstrate DriveVA's superior performance and improved video-trajectory consistency, highlighting it as the benchmark to beat.
citations.title: From forecasting to planning: Policy world model for collaborative state-action prediction

citations.fullCitation: Peebles, W., Xie, S.: Scalable diffusion models with transformers. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF international conference on computer vision. pp. 4195–4205 (2023)
citations.justification: This paper introduces the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture, which is the core component of DriveVA's novel decoder. DriveVA's central contribution of jointly generating video and action tokens in a shared latent space is implemented using a DiT, making this citation crucial for understanding the paper's methodology.
citations.title: Scalable diffusion models with transformers

citations.fullCitation: Li, Y., Shang, S., Liu, W., Zhan, B., Wang, H., Wang, Y., Chen, Y., Wang, X., An, Y., Tang, C., et al.: Drivevla-w0: World models amplify data scaling law in autonomous driving. arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.12796 (2025)
citations.justification: This work is cited as a major contemporary world model-based method and is used as a baseline for comparison in both closed-loop performance on NAVSIM and zero-shot transfer to Bench2Drive. It represents a highly relevant approach that also integrates world models, making it an important benchmark for contextualizing DriveVA's performance and contributions.
citations.title: Drivevla-w0: World models amplify data scaling law in autonomous driving

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "DriveVA: Video Action Models are Zero-Shot Drivers."

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by:
*   **Mengmeng Liu** (University of Twente)
*   **Diankun Zhang** (Xiaomi EV)
*   **Jiuming Liu** (University of Cambridge)
*   **Jianfeng Cui** (Xiaomi EV)
*   **Hongwei Xie** (Xiaomi EV)
*   **Guang Chen** (Xiaomi EV)
*   **Hangjun Ye** (Xiaomi EV)
*   **Michael Ying Yang** (University of Bath)
*   **Francesco Nex** (University of Twente)
*   **Hao Cheng** (University of Twente, corresponding author)

The primary affiliations are the University of Twente, Xiaomi EV, University of Cambridge, and University of Bath.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Generalization is a fundamental challenge in autonomous driving, requiring systems to perform robustly in unseen scenarios, varying sensor domains, and diverse environmental conditions. Traditional autonomous driving models often struggle with novel traffic patterns, road layouts, and sensor configurations, which are common in real-world deployment.

Recent advancements in large-scale pre-trained models have influenced the development of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in autonomous driving. These models leverage pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) as backbones, fine-tuned on driving-specific trajectory data. While VLAs can reduce task-specific data requirements and transfer semantic knowledge, their pretraining on static image-text pairs limits their ability to capture essential spatiotemporal dynamics and causal interaction patterns needed for robust closed-loop planning. Consequently, their zero-shot generalization across datasets remains limited. Approaches to address VLA generalization have included targeted data construction for corner cases and structured expert modeling for long-tail behaviors, but these often rely on predefined scenario categories or skill partitions, restricting true cross-dataset transfer.

Concurrently, large-scale video generation models have demonstrated capabilities in synthesizing temporally coherent future predictions under flexible conditioning, capturing realistic motion patterns and physically plausible scene dynamics from vast video corpora. These models inherently encode rich priors over real-world temporal evolution. This development raises the question of whether large video generation models can serve as a foundation for generalizable autonomous driving world models.

Existing world-model-based planning methods, which aim to improve driving decisions by forecasting future scene evolution, fall into two main categories: latent-dynamics models (e.g., LAW, World4Drive) and visually predictive models (e.g., Epona, DriveVLA-W0). Latent-dynamics models typically treat the world model as an auxiliary module, while visually predictive approaches often use visual prediction as an auxiliary signal or a loosely coupled component. A significant limitation across both categories is the inconsistency between visual and action rollouts, as video imagination and trajectory generation are often modeled separately or with weak coupling. This can lead to mismatches where executed actions deviate from the visually imagined future.

DriveVA addresses these limitations by integrating the spatiotemporal priors from large-scale video generation models directly into an end-to-end planning framework. It seeks to overcome the generalization barrier of existing world models and the video-trajectory inconsistency by jointly modeling future visual forecasts and action sequences within a shared latent generative process. This approach aligns with emerging VAM-style (Video Action Model) directions that leverage web-scale video data to infer physical dynamics, offering a more scalable path toward generalization beyond fixed action templates and benchmark-specific data distributions.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this work is to develop an autonomous driving world model, named DriveVA, that exhibits strong generalization capabilities across diverse, unseen scenarios, sensor domains, and environmental conditions, while also ensuring consistency between visual scene evolution and planned vehicle actions.

The motivation for DriveVA stems from several limitations observed in current autonomous driving systems:
1.  **Limited Generalization in Existing World Models:** Traditional world-model-based planning methods often struggle with generalization across different datasets and sensor configurations. The world knowledge acquired from one dataset frequently does not transfer effectively to another, posing a challenge for real-world deployment where unseen traffic patterns, novel road layouts, and varied sensor setups are common.
2.  **Inconsistency Between Visual and Action Rollouts:** Many existing planning methods, including those based on world models, typically model visual imagination and trajectory generation as separate or only loosely coupled processes. This decoupled approach can lead to inconsistencies where the vehicle's planned actions do not align coherently with the predicted visual evolution of the scene, resulting in poor video-trajectory consistency during visual imagination and potentially unsafe or inefficient driving behaviors.
3.  **Untapped Potential of Large-Scale Video Generation Models:** Recent advancements in large-scale video generation models have demonstrated their ability to learn and synthesize realistic motion patterns and physically plausible scene dynamics from massive video corpora. These models possess rich spatiotemporal and causal priors that are highly relevant for understanding and predicting the dynamic evolution of driving environments. The research is motivated by the hypothesis that these powerful priors could be adapted to enhance the generalization and planning capabilities of autonomous driving systems, a potential not fully exploited by existing VLA models whose pretraining often focuses on static image-text pairs.
4.  **Necessity of Video-Level Supervision for Planning Gains:** The authors hypothesize that dense video-level supervision is crucial for significant planning improvements, rather than merely treating video prediction as an auxiliary loss. They suggest that planning benefits substantially when predicted actions are compelled to remain consistent with the imagined future scene, requiring a tighter integration than commonly found in cascaded pipelines.

By addressing these motivations, DriveVA aims to create a more robust, generalizable, and coherent autonomous driving system.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

DriveVA is designed as a unified video-action world model that integrates large-scale video priors with end-to-end planning, using dense video-level supervision. The core approach involves jointly decoding future visual forecasts (videos) and action sequences (trajectories) within a shared latent generative process.

**Problem Formulation:**
Given a language instruction, a history observation buffer (e.g., *m* frames), and the current ego state (velocity components), DriveVA aims to jointly predict:
1.  An action chunk consisting of *K* future actions (3D vectors: x, y position, and yaw angle).
2.  A future video clip of *N* frames, represented by their latent forms, depicting the anticipated visual evolution aligned with the predicted actions.
This process follows a rolling-horizon setup, where after executing an action chunk, the observation buffer is updated, and the prediction process repeats.

**Joint Video–Action Modeling:**
DriveVA models the joint distribution of future video imaginations and action chunks conditioned on historical observations, language instructions, and the current ego state. This formulation conceptually unifies video continuation and Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM)-style action grounding within a single end-to-end model. Instead of separate models for video prediction and inverse dynamics, DriveVA optimizes a single model, encouraging tight video–action alignment through deep cross-modal integration.

**Data Preprocessing:**
*   **Text Instruction Encoding:** A frozen text encoder from the Wan2.2-TI2V-5B model encodes language instructions into fixed-length latent token sequences. These tokens are injected into the backbone via a cross-attention mechanism.
*   **Video Causal VAE:** A 3D-causal VAE encoder from Wan2.2-TI2V-5B is used to encode video clips into temporally downsampled latent sequences. The causal design ensures that the first latent feature depends only on the first frame, extended to a history observation buffer for video continuation. During inference, only the history buffer is encoded to condition the generation of future latents.

**Consistent Video-Action Generation Architecture:**
*   **Backbone:** Wan2.2-TI2V-5B, a large pre-trained video generation model, serves as the foundation.
*   **Input Tokenization:** Each visual latent from the history buffer is raster-flattened and projected into the model's dimension. The current ego state is embedded into state tokens using a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), and each future action is also embedded into a single token via an MLP.
*   **Input Structure:** The model input is divided into a noise-free condition block (history visual latents, ego state) and a generative target block (future video latents, future action tokens). The condition block remains fixed during both training and inference.
*   **Unified Diffusion Transformer (DiT) Decoder:** A DiT-based decoder is employed to jointly predict the conditional velocity field for the generative target block. Critically, future video latents and action tokens are placed in a *shared latent space* and decoded by a *single DiT*. This enables self-attention between video and action tokens, facilitating bidirectional interaction and mutual conditioning during denoising, which leads to stronger video–trajectory consistency.

**Flow Matching Objective:**
The generative process is formulated using flow matching. A conditional flow is instantiated over the generative target block, conditioned on the fixed context and text tokens. The model is trained to regress the target velocity field of a linear interpolation between noise and the clean target tokens, using a standard flow-matching regression loss. At inference, generation starts from Gaussian noise, and the learned velocity field is integrated to obtain the clean latent representations of future videos and actions.

**Progressive Video Continuation:**
To maintain long-duration consistency and enable recurrent decision-making, DriveVA incorporates a video continuation module. This module progressively rolls out future video clips by recursively feeding the newly generated visual latents back into the conditioning buffer for subsequent predictions, effectively chaining short prediction windows into long-horizon rollouts.

**Training Details:**
Training is performed on the NAVSIM v1 benchmark. Each training sample comprises 4 history frames and 8 future frames at 2 FPS, with a resolution of 832 × 480. The optimizer is AdamW, with a learning rate of 10<sup>-4</sup>, weight decay of 0.01, and bf16 mixed-precision training. A linear warm-up is applied, followed by a constant learning rate. The training objective combines the flow-matching loss for future-frame generation with a trajectory prediction loss. During inference, efficient closed-loop planning is achieved using only 2 sampling steps for flow-based sampling.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research provides several key findings regarding DriveVA's performance and capabilities:

**5.1. Closed-Loop Performance on NAVSIM v1:**
*   DriveVA achieved a closed-loop performance of 90.9 PDM score on the NAVSIM v1 benchmark. This result surpasses both traditional end-to-end methods (e.g., DiffusionDrive at 88.1 PDMS) and other recent world-model-based approaches (e.g., PWM at 88.1 PDMS, Epona at 86.2 PDMS, DriveVLA-W0 at 87.2 PDMS).
*   Notably, DriveVA achieved this performance using only front-view camera images, whereas some comparative methods (e.g., WoTE) utilize multi-modal inputs, suggesting high efficiency in leveraging visual data.
*   The improvements are attributed to the unified video-action formulation, which ensures better alignment between the model's visual imagination and its planned actions.

**5.2. Zero-Shot Cross-Domain Generalization:**
*   **nuScenes:** When trained solely on NAVSIM and evaluated zero-shot on nuScenes (without any fine-tuning), DriveVA demonstrated substantial improvements. It reduced the average L2 error by 78.9% and the average collision rate by 83.3% compared to PWM, a state-of-the-art world-model-based planner. DriveVA achieved an average L2 error of 0.84m and a collision rate of 0.06% on nuScenes. The L2 error reductions were consistent across horizons (84.0% at 1s, 80.6% at 2s, and 76.2% at 3s).
*   **Bench2Drive (CARLA):** For real-to-simulation transfer (NAVSIM-trained to Bench2Drive), DriveVA reduced the average L2 error by 52.5% (from 2.80m to 1.33m) and the average collision rate by 52.4% (from 3.76% to 1.79%) compared to PWM. It also surpassed DriveVLA-W0, reducing its average L2 error by 55.7% and collision rate by 29.0%.
*   The results indicate that DriveVA can achieve better performance than methods fine-tuned on nuScenes, even without explicit training on the target dataset, suggesting genuinely transferable planning priors learned through unified video-action modeling.

**5.3. Video–Trajectory Consistency Analysis:**
*   **Quantitative Verification (DPVO-based):** Using an independent visual odometry system (DPVO) to reconstruct trajectories from both ground-truth and generated future videos, the average L2 error between the model's predicted trajectories and the DPVO-reconstructed trajectories from its *generated videos* was consistently low (0.15m across NAVSIM and nuScenes). This quantitative evidence confirms that the motion implied by DriveVA's generated videos is well-aligned with its predicted trajectories.
*   **Qualitative Visualization:** Visualizations across various scenarios (lane-change, right-turn, straight-driving) on nuScenes showed that the trajectory reconstructed from the generated future video closely followed the model-predicted trajectory. This supports that DriveVA couples future visual imagination and planning behavior in a geometrically consistent manner.
*   The comparative analysis with PWM indicated that DriveVA maintains strong video-trajectory consistency even under domain shifts, whereas PWM often exhibited mismatches.

**5.4. Simulation-Enhanced Real-World Transferring:**
*   Joint training of DriveVA with simulation data (Bench2Drive) alongside NAVSIM data further improved performance on real-world benchmarks. This combined training enhanced planning metrics like TTC (Time-To-Collision) to 98.7 and PDMS (Planning Driver Metrics Score) to 90.9, suggesting that diverse, high-quality corner-case scenarios from simulation can provide complementary supervision and improve real-world closed-loop planning.

**5.5. Ablation Studies:**
*   **Video Loss:** Explicit video supervision was found to be critical, with its removal causing a significant drop in PDMS from 90.9 to 71.4.
*   **Video Continuation:** This module was important for long-horizon consistency, as its removal degraded PDMS to 84.6.
*   **Causal Masking Strategy:** Bidirectional interaction between video and action tokens in the unified DiT decoder resulted in better performance (90.9 PDMS) than a causal masking strategy (90.1 PDMS), indicating mutual conditioning is beneficial.
*   **Dual-Prediction Strategy (Action-Only):** An action-only variant (without joint video prediction) resulted in a dramatic performance drop (PDMS 47.0), underscoring the necessity of explicitly modeling future videos as a coupled prediction target.
*   **Sampling Steps:** DriveVA achieved near-optimal performance with only 2 sampling steps at inference, indicating computational efficiency.
*   **Prediction Time Horizon:** Predicting 8 future frames (aligned with a 4-second action chunk) yielded the best results (PDMS 90.9), while shorter (4 frames) or longer (12 frames) rollouts led to reduced performance.
*   **Training Strategy:** Full fine-tuning of the pre-trained backbone achieved the best performance (PDMS 90.9), significantly outperforming LoRA adaptation (PDMS 74.9) and training from scratch (PDMS 62.9).
*   **Model Size:** While larger backbones (14B LoRA) showed potential over smaller ones (5B LoRA), full fine-tuning of the 5B model surpassed the 14B LoRA, indicating the adaptation strategy's importance.

**5.6. Failure Cases:**
*   Qualitative analysis of failure cases showed that even when DriveVA's future prediction deviated from the ground-truth behavior (e.g., incorrect stops at intersections or for cyclists), the predicted trajectory remained consistent with the *generated* future video. This suggests that the internal consistency mechanism operates effectively, even if the predicted future mode itself is incorrect, highlighting areas for further improvement in visual causal reasoning.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The DriveVA research introduces a novel approach to autonomous driving world modeling with several significant implications:

**Advancement in Generalization:** The most notable contribution is DriveVA's demonstrated zero-shot generalization capabilities. Its ability to transfer robustly from NAVSIM to unseen real-world nuScenes and simulated Bench2Drive environments without target-domain fine-tuning represents a substantial step toward addressing a central challenge in autonomous driving. This suggests that the model learns fundamental, transferable priors about driving dynamics and scene evolution, rather than merely interpolating observed patterns.

**Enhanced Video–Trajectory Consistency:** By jointly modeling future video imagination and action sequences within a shared latent generative process, DriveVA achieves a high degree of consistency between its predicted visual future and its planned ego-vehicle trajectory. This integrated approach alleviates the mismatches common in cascaded or loosely coupled planning pipelines, leading to more coherent and interpretable decision-making.

**Leveraging Large Video Generation Models:** DriveVA effectively utilizes the rich spatiotemporal and causal priors embedded in large pre-trained video generation models. This strategy shows that these powerful general-purpose models can serve as foundational components for embodied AI tasks like autonomous driving, accelerating development and potentially reducing the need for extensive domain-specific data collection for initial training.

**Robustness and Reliability:** The improved video–trajectory consistency and strong generalization contribute to a more robust and reliable planning system. In real-world deployment, where unexpected scenarios and domain shifts are inevitable, a model that can maintain coherent planning with minimal prior exposure to specific conditions is safer and more adaptable.

**Efficiency:** The finding that DriveVA achieves near-optimal closed-loop performance with as few as two sampling steps at inference time indicates computational efficiency, which is critical for real-time autonomous driving applications.

**New Research Direction:** This work casts new insight into the autonomous driving research domain by demonstrating that video action models can function as zero-shot drivers. It suggests a promising avenue for future research into developing increasingly generalizable and performant autonomous driving systems built upon powerful generative video models.

**Potential Real-World Impact:** The ability to generalize to unseen scenarios and domains is crucial for the widespread adoption and safety of autonomous vehicles. DriveVA's advancements in generalization and consistency could lead to more robust autonomous driving systems that require less extensive and costly fine-tuning for deployment in new geographical areas or under varied conditions, potentially accelerating the development and deployment of safe and reliable self-driving technology.
overview:
Autonomous driving systems have historically struggled with generalization—the ability to perform reliably in environments, weather conditions, or sensor configurations they have never seen before. While current approaches like Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have improved the understanding of driving scenes by pre-training on large-scale text and image datasets, they often lack a deep understanding of how scenes evolve over time. This leads to a disconnect between what the model "imagines" will happen and what the vehicle actually does.

DriveVA addresses these challenges by reimagining the driving task as a joint video-generation and trajectory-planning problem. By leveraging the power of large-scale video generation models, DriveVA functions as a "Video Action Model" (VAM) that can drive zero-shot in unseen domains. It does this by ensuring that the vehicle's planned actions are mathematically and logically consistent with a predicted future video of the scene.

![DriveVA Overview: Unified Video-Action Modeling and Zero-Shot Generalization](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04198v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: DriveVA integrates spatiotemporal priors from video generation models to achieve consistent future prediction and planning across diverse datasets.*

## The Challenge of Generalization and Consistency

The primary bottleneck for modern autonomous driving is the "domain gap." A model trained on the sunny streets of California may fail when deployed in a rainy European city or when its camera resolution changes. Traditional models are often benchmark-specific; they memorize the patterns of a particular dataset (like NAVSIM or nuScenes) but fail to transfer that knowledge to others.

Furthermore, existing "world models"—systems that try to predict the future to improve planning—often separate video prediction from action planning. They might use a video generator to imagine the future and a separate planning head to decide where to go. This decoupling frequently results in inconsistencies: the video might show the car turning right while the planner suggests going straight, or vice-versa. DriveVA seeks to solve this by unifying these two tasks into a single generative process where video frames and action coordinates are treated as part of the same data sequence.

## Unified Video-Action Architecture

At its core, DriveVA is built upon a large-scale pre-trained video generation model (Wan2.2-TI2V-5B). The architecture is designed to handle multiple streams of information—visual history, language instructions, and the current vehicle state—to predict both a future video and a future trajectory.

![DriveVA Architecture: Unified Diffusion Transformer and Progressive Continuation](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04198v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The model uses a shared latent space where future video tokens and action tokens interact through a Unified Diffusion Transformer.*

The process begins with **Conditional Signal Embedding**:
1.  **History Observations ($O_h$):** A buffer of $m$ past frames is encoded into a latent space using a 3D-causal Variational Autoencoder (VAE).
2.  **Language Instructions ($L$):** Optional text prompts (e.g., "Turn right at the intersection") are encoded into tokens.
3.  **Ego State ($s_0$):** The current velocity and orientation of the vehicle are embedded as state tokens.

The unique contribution of DriveVA is its **Unified Diffusion Transformer (DiT)**. Unlike standard models that predict actions and pixels separately, DriveVA places future video latents and action tokens into a shared sequence. During the denoising process of the diffusion model, the video tokens and action tokens can "talk" to each other through self-attention. This bidirectional interaction forces the model to ensure that the predicted trajectory ($A$) and the predicted future video ($F$) are mutually conditioning each other.

The goal is to model the joint distribution $P(F, A | O_h, L, s_0)$, where $F$ is a sequence of $N$ future frames and $A$ is a chunk of $K$ future actions (3D vectors containing coordinates and yaw angles).

## Methodology: Flow Matching for Planning

DriveVA utilizes a generative technique called **Flow Matching**. In this framework, the model learns to map a simple distribution of noise to the complex distribution of realistic driving videos and trajectories. The training objective, $\mathcal{L}_{FM}$, is defined as:

$$\mathcal{L}_{FM} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon} [ \| v_\theta(x_t, t | c) - u_t(x_t) \|^2 ]$$

In this equation:
- $v_\theta$ represents the velocity field predicted by the network.
- $x_t = (1 - t)x_0 + tx_1$ is a linear interpolation between noise ($x_0$) and the target clean data ($x_1$).
- $t \in [0, 1]$ is the time step of the diffusion process.
- $u_t(x_t) = x_1 - x_0$ is the target velocity the model aims to regress.
- $c = \{O_h, L, s_0\}$ represents the condition set including history, language, and state.

By training on this objective with high-resolution video ($832 \times 480$), the model learns the physics of the world—how objects move, how light changes, and how the ego-vehicle's movement affects the visual scene. During inference, DriveVA can generate a coherent future and an accompanying plan in as few as 2 sampling steps, making it efficient enough for real-time consideration.

## Zero-Shot Generalization Results

The most significant finding of this work is DriveVA's performance on datasets it was never trained on. Typically, a model trained on the NAVSIM benchmark would see its performance drop significantly when tested on nuScenes or the CARLA simulator (Bench2Drive).

DriveVA, however, demonstrated a high level of "zero-shot" transfer:
- **nuScenes:** Without any fine-tuning, DriveVA reduced the average collision rate by 83.3% compared to PWM, a leading world-model planner. Its average position error (L2) was reduced by 78.9%.
- **Bench2Drive (Real-to-Sim):** Moving from real-world data to a simulation environment is notoriously difficult. DriveVA reduced position errors by 52.5% compared to PWM and outperformed DriveVLA-W0, showing that its learned priors are robust to changes in graphics and physics.

This zero-shot capability suggests that DriveVA is not just memorizing maps; it is learning the "language of motion" and the physical constraints of driving that apply regardless of the specific camera or location.

![Zero-Shot Performance on nuScenes and CARLA](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04198v1/x3.png)
*Figure 3: DriveVA exhibits superior generalization across real-world and simulated domains compared to existing world models.*

## Ensuring Video-Trajectory Consistency

A common failure mode for world-model planners is the "hallucination" of a future that does not match the planned action. DriveVA's joint modeling ensures high consistency. To prove this, the researchers used an independent Visual Odometry system (DPVO) to calculate the vehicle's movement *based solely on the video DriveVA generated*. 

They found that the trajectory calculated from the pixels matched the model's predicted mathematical coordinates with an average error of only 0.15 meters. This confirms that when DriveVA predicts a right turn, the "imagined" video actually depicts a right turn with the correct perspective shift, and the "planned" trajectory follows that exact curve.

![Consistency Visualization across Lane-Changes and Turns](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04198v1/x4.png)
*Figure 4: Qualitative results showing that predicted trajectories (blue) align closely with ground truth (red) and visual predictions (yellow/cyan).*

## Limitations and Failure Cases

Despite its strong performance, DriveVA is not perfect. The researchers identified failure cases where the model's visual causal reasoning failed. For instance, in some scenarios involving cyclists or complex intersections, the model might predict an unnecessary stop. 

Interestingly, even in these failure cases, the **consistency** remained: if the model incorrectly predicted that the car should stop in the video, its planned trajectory also stopped. This indicates that the unified architecture is working as intended—the "brain" (planner) and the "eyes" (video generator) are always in agreement, even if the model's overall interpretation of the scene is flawed.

![Failure Case Analysis: Consistency persists even when the prediction is incorrect](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04198v1/x9.png)
*Figure 5: In cases of incorrect stop predictions, the predicted trajectory still aligns with the generated video, highlighting the model's internal consistency.*

## Significance for the Future of Autonomous Driving

DriveVA represents a shift toward more generalizable autonomous agents. By treating driving as a spatiotemporal generation task rather than a simple regression or classification task, the model captures the rich dynamics of the real world. 

The key takeaways from this research are:
1.  **Unified Modeling is Vital:** Jointly predicting video and actions in a shared latent space is far more effective than training them separately or using one as a secondary task.
2.  **Video Priors are Powerful:** Large-scale video generation models contain "common sense" about physics and motion that can be directly harnessed for driving.
3.  **Generalization is Possible:** Zero-shot transfer across datasets is achievable when a model focuses on the fundamental consistency between vision and action.

DriveVA provides a scalable path forward for autonomous systems that can operate safely in the "long tail" of rare and unseen real-world scenarios, moving us closer to truly universal autonomous drivers.

keyInsights: Integrating spatiotemporal priors from large-scale video generation models directly into an end-to-end planning framework is crucial for achieving strong zero-shot generalization in autonomous driving.
Jointly modeling future visual imaginations and action sequences within a unified latent space significantly enhances video-trajectory consistency and overall planning performance.
Dense video-level supervision is essential for substantial improvements in planning and for learning truly transferable priors, rather than treating video prediction as merely an auxiliary loss.
originalProblem: Autonomous driving models often lack generalization across diverse, unseen scenarios, sensor domains, and environmental conditions, hindering real-world deployment.
Existing world-model-based planning methods frequently exhibit inconsistency between their visual predictions and planned vehicle actions, leading to misaligned behaviors.

Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, typically pretrained on static image-text pairs, do not adequately capture the spatiotemporal dynamics and causal interaction patterns required for robust closed-loop planning and cross-dataset transfer.
results: DriveVA achieved a closed-loop performance of 90.9 PDM score on the NAVSIM v1 benchmark, outperforming existing end-to-end and world-model-based methods.

The model demonstrated strong zero-shot generalization capabilities, reducing average L2 error by 78.9% and collision rate by 83.3% on nuScenes, and L2 error by 52.5% and collision rate by 52.4% on Bench2Drive, compared to previous state-of-the-art.

Quantitative analysis using an independent visual odometry system (DPVO) confirmed high video-trajectory consistency, with an average L2 error of 0.15m between predicted trajectories and those reconstructed from generated videos.
solution: DriveVA unifies video and action modeling by jointly decoding future visual forecasts (videos) and action sequences (trajectories) within a shared latent generative process.
It leverages a large pre-trained video generation model (Wan2.2-TI2V-5B) as its backbone, integrating its rich spatiotemporal priors into an end-to-end planning framework.

A single Diffusion Transformer (DiT) decoder places future video latents and action tokens in a shared latent space, enabling bidirectional interaction and mutual conditioning to ensure strong video-trajectory consistency.
summary.summary: DriveVA, an autonomous driving world model developed by researchers including those from the University of Twente and Xiaomi EV, jointly predicts future visual scene evolution and vehicle actions within a shared latent space. The model achieved a 90.9 PDM score on the NAVSIM v1 benchmark and demonstrated zero-shot generalization, reducing average L2 error by 78.9% and collision rate by 83.3% on nuScenes compared to previous state-of-the-art.

title: DriveVA: Video Action Models are Zero-Shot Drivers
